coredata.dc:title: Development and optimization of hybrid heat dissipation system for lithium-ion battery packs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xuguang
Liu, Yang
Halbig, Michael
Singh, Mrityunjay
Almansour, Amjad
Zheng, Yi
coredata.dc:description: This study presents the development and optimization of an advanced hybrid heat dissipation system for lithium-ion battery packs designed explicitly for drone applications. The system employs a novel battery capsule filled with a phase change material (PCM) compound enhanced with 2 % Huber nano-carbon, demonstrating superior thermal conductivity and stability. The capsules were manufactured using 3D printing technology, with paraffin wax and carbon black powders as the PCM components. Thermal simulations and experimental results indicated that the oval-shaped capsules with a major/minor axis ratio of 17.2/14.2 mm provided the most effective heat dissipation, maintaining the battery within a safe operating temperature range. Comparative studies revealed that the PCM-filled capsules outperformed the efficiency of air-filled and pure paraffin wax-filled capsules in thermal management. The system’s dual approach, combining passive PCM cooling with active air cooling, significantly extended the battery’s operational life and safety by ensuring optimal temperature regulation. This research highlights the potential for integrating nano-carbon-enhanced PCMs in high-performance, temperature-sensitive devices, paving the way for broader applications in electric vehicles, portable electronics, and advanced energy storage systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3D-printed capsule
Hybrid cooling
Heat dissipation
Lithium-ion battery
Nano-carbon
Phase change material

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project is partially supported by the National Aeronautics and Space Administration Glenn Research Center Faculty Fellowship and the National Science Foundation through grant number CBET-1941743. The authors would like to acknowledge Dr. Jon Goldsby, Dr. Zachary Tuchfeld, and Dr. Meelad Ranaiefar at the NASA Glenn Research Center for their discussion of the 3D-printed battery capsules and heat dissipation data analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Configuration of battery capsules. (a) Perspective view of a cylindrical battery capsule. (b) A sequential view of a thermal process within a battery capsule, depicted across four distinct stages. (c) Cross-section of a cylindrical battery capsule. (d) Cross-section of an oval, cylindrical battery capsule.
PCM layer temperature profiles of cylindrical and oval battery capsules with varied dimensions under left-to-right airflow conditions. All measurements are presented in millimeters (mm).
(a) Experimental setup for the hybrid heat dissipation system. (b) The sectional view at the center of the battery capsule details the placement of thermocouples about the heater and sheaths.

(a) Heating and cooling rates of PCMs enhanced with varying Cabot and Huber carbon black ratios. The Mix marks on the x-axis, including Cabot and Huber, have a fixed mass ratio of 54.5/45.5. The temperature change rate per minute is shown on the y-axis. A lower rate is preferable for PCMs for efficient thermal energy management. The experiments were conducted at 70 °C. (b) Temperature profiles for PCMs with varying concentrations of Huber carbon black are shown as percentages. The graph is divided into three stages: charging, phase change, and steady state, indicated by the background color. Each line corresponds to a PCM with a specific Huber carbon black concentration, ranging from 1 % to 5 %. As time progresses, all samples initially experience a rapid increase in temperature. A gradual rise during the Phase Change, and eventually reach a Steady State, where the temperature stabilizes. The curves suggest that the thermal response of PCM and the time it takes to get a steady state vary with the concentration of carbon black.

Temperature rate (°C/min) against the geometric radius ratio (
r
ma , o
/
r
mi , o
) for various components within a battery capsule system, differentiated by wax and Huber-based PCM materials. The geometric radius ratio represents the ratio of the major axis radii over the minor axis radii of the outer sheath, which reflects the thickness of the PCM layers. The temperature rate indicates how quickly the temperature of each component rises or falls over time.
Comparative analysis of air cooling performance: (a) PCM-filled capsule vs. air-filled capsule, and (b) PCM-filled capsule vs. wax-filled capsule.
Thermal camera images at six different stages for wax-filled and PCM-filled capsules.
Thermal properties and operating parameters of the battery system.
Material Properties for Waxes and Carbon Blacks.
Thermal properties of Huber Carbon Black-based PCMs.

row.entry:
Thermal conductivity
W / m Â · K

head.ce:title: Development and optimization of hybrid heat dissipation system for lithium-ion battery packs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents the development and optimization of an advanced hybrid heat dissipation system for lithium-ion battery packs designed explicitly for drone applications. The system employs a novel battery capsule filled with a phase change material (PCM) compound enhanced with 2 % Huber nano-carbon, demonstrating superior thermal conductivity and stability. The capsules were manufactured using 3D printing technology, with paraffin wax and carbon black powders as the PCM components. Thermal simulations and experimental results indicated that the oval-shaped capsules with a major/minor axis ratio of 17.2/14.2 mm provided the most effective heat dissipation, maintaining the battery within a safe operating temperature range. Comparative studies revealed that the PCM-filled capsules outperformed the efficiency of air-filled and pure paraffin wax-filled capsules in thermal management. The system’s dual approach, combining passive PCM cooling with active air cooling, significantly extended the battery’s operational life and safety by ensuring optimal temperature regulation. This research highlights the potential for integrating nano-carbon-enhanced PCMs in high-performance, temperature-sensitive devices, paving the way for broader applications in electric vehicles, portable electronics, and advanced energy storage systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The integration of lithium-ion batteries into modern transportation systems, particularly drones, signifies a substantial advancement due to their high energy storage density and extended service life. However, these batteries are inherently sensitive to temperature fluctuations, generating significant heat during operation and impacting their efficiency, longevity, and safety. Effective thermal management systems are thus imperative to ensure that batteries operate within safe temperature thresholds [1,2]. Traditional thermal management systems, divided into active and passive cooling methods, present certain limitations. Active cooling methods, such as forced air convection and liquid cooling, are often complex, heavy, and energy-consuming [3,4]. Conversely, passive cooling methods utilizing phase change materials (PCMs) are favored for their high latent heat capacity and minimal volume change, making them an efficient and cost-effective solution [5–7].

This study introduces an advanced hybrid heat dissipation system for lithium-ion batteries, employing a novel design of battery capsules filled with a phase change material (PCM) compound enhanced with nano-carbon. This design of the battery capsule allows for quick replacement of batteries, which is ideal for drone applications where operational efficiency is paramount. Incorporating nano-carbon in the PCM enhances thermal conductivity and extends the safe usage time of the batteries by maintaining them within an optimal temperature range. This dual approach of combining active air cooling with passive PCM cooling balances rapid heat dissipation and temperature regulation [8,9]. Compared to metal particles, such as silver nanoparticles, nano-carbon offers several advantages. While metals like silver provide high thermal conductivity, they are prone to oxidation, which can reduce their efficiency and longevity, especially at the nanoscale [10]. Nano-carbon materials, on the other hand, offer excellent thermal conductivity and exhibit superior stability and compatibility with organic PCMs. Additionally, nano-carbon particles are generally lighter and less expensive than metal nanoparticles, making them more suitable for applications requiring lightweight and cost-effective solutions, such as drones [11–13].

Recent studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of integrating conductive additives like carbon black into PCMs, significantly enhancing the cooling system's thermal conductivity and overall efficiency [14,15]. The hybrid cooling system developed in this study leverages this approach, combining paraffin wax and carbon black powders with a primary particle size of 19 nm. Paraffin wax was chosen for its melting point of 40 °C, below the battery's optimal operating temperature of 45 °C, providing effective heat dissipation. Thermal simulations and experiments revealed that a mass ratio concentration of 2 % nano-carbon PCM exhibited the best effectiveness, ensuring the lithium-ion battery remained within the safe temperature region during operation [16,17]. The potential future applications of this design extend beyond drones to other high-performance, temperature-sensitive devices such as electric vehicles (EVs) and portable electronic devices. The high thermal conductivity and stability of nano-carbon-enhanced PCMs make them suitable for these applications, where efficient thermal management is critical for safety and performance [18,19]. Additionally, the development of smart wearable devices and advanced energy storage systems can benefit from the superior thermal regulation properties of nano-carbon PCMs, further broadening the scope of their application [20–22].

This study employed simulation and experimental methods to explore the efficacy of the compound material in battery thermal management systems. The 3D-printed battery capsules, filled with paraffin wax and carbon black powders, demonstrated superior heat dissipation performance, especially with an oval-shaped capsule design. This design enhances heat transfer efficiency, making it a robust solution for high-performance lithium-ion battery systems used in drones. The findings provide valuable insights into designing efficient and cost-effective thermal management systems, highlighting the potential of PCM in enhancing thermal dissipation and extending the operational life and safety of lithium-ion batteries [23,24].

The main energy storage unit of the study is the 18650 lithium-ion battery. The battery capsules are manufactured by 3D printing. The material of choice was Markforged’s Onyx© filament, an extremely strong compound reinforced with chopped carbon fibers. The design incorporates a void space within the capsule dedicated to the PCM layer, which remains empty during the 3D printing. The void space is then filled with a PCM layer of a specially formulated compound containing paraffin wax-impregnated carbon black powder. Carbon black enhances the thermal response of paraffin wax, optimizing the system's thermal management. The compound can improve the system's thermal conductivity and heat distribution efficiency.

The configuration of battery capsules and the integration of a PCM layer are illustrated in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1c reveals a cylindrical battery capsule structure. The outer sheath contacts with the ambient environment at the outer surface (
r
os , o
) and contacts the PCM at the inner surface (
r
os , i
). The inner sheath contacts with the battery core at the inner surface (
r
is , i
) and contacts the PCM at the outer surface (
r
is , o
). The PCM layer is the region between the outer surface of the inner sheath and the inner surface of the outer sheath (from
r
is , o
t o
r
os , i
). Fig. 1d portrays an oval-shaped capsule design. Here, the outer sheath's major (
r
ma , o
) and minor (
r
mi , o
) external axes contact with the ambient air, while the major (
r
ma , i
) and minor (
r
mi , i
) internal axes interface with the PCM layer. The inner sheath region is consistent with the configuration delineated in Fig. 1c.

A comprehensive time-dependent heat transfer analysis chosen in COMSOL encompassed laminar flow within solid components and fluids for cylindrical and oval-shaped thermal capsules designed for a single battery. The simulation chose a thickness of 0.5 mm for the inner sheath (the difference between
r
is , i
and
r
is , o
) to facilitate adequate heat conduction from the exterior surface of the battery. The thickness for the capsule's outer sheath (the difference between
r
os , i
and
r
os , o
) was also set at 0.5 mm. This dimension was chosen to meet the printing capabilities of the Markforged 3D Printer.

The representation of thermal activity within a battery capsule through four distinct stages is depicted in Fig. 1b. The color depth represents the temperature, the arrow indicates the direction of heat flux, and the arrow's size corresponds to the heat flux's magnitude. The initial charging phase is observed in Stage 1, where thermal energy is radiated homogeneously outward and transferred from the battery to the outer sheath of the capsule. In the phase transition stages, shown in Stage 2 and Stage 3, thermal energy is absorbed by the PCM. During this phase, the PCM stores energy as latent heat, which slows the temperature rise of the battery and keeps it within a safe temperature range, with minimal temperature change in the PCM itself. The arrows for heat flux during this stage indicate energy absorption by the PCM. Finally, Stage 4 illustrates the steady-state phase, where a balance is maintained between the heat generated by the battery and the heat dissipated to the surrounding environment. The uniformly spaced outer arrows represent that the system has achieved thermal stability. This final stage demonstrates the system's ability to manage thermal loads without overheating, maintaining battery performance and longevity.

The ambient airflow is directed from left to right. In Fig. 2 a, for the cylindrical capsule, the turbulence in the wake region increases significantly as the cylindrical radius increases. This turbulence is represented by a pattern of varying shades of color left side of the cylinder due to increased velocity fluctuations and vortex structures in the capsule's wake region. A larger radius results in a wider wake region and more pronounced turbulence due to a thicker boundary layer separating the object. In Fig. 2b and Fig. 2c, different horizontal and vertical axis ratios are analyzed. In Fig. 3 b, there is an investigation into an oval-shaped capsule that effectively decreases the sharpness of flow separation when compared to its cylindrical counterpart, resulting in a smoother wake region. However, turbulence characteristics remain similar to those observed in cylindrical capsules. Fig. 3c showcases a more streamlined oval-shaped capsule, which optimizes flow attachment even further in the wake region. This model significantly reduces turbulence and wake size while minimizing airflow disturbance compared to the previous two shapes. Finally, Fig. 2d shows an oval-shaped capsule with a longer major radius (
r
ma , i
). Increasing the length of the major radius (
r
ma , i
) can stabilize the flow and reduce turbulence in the capsule’s wake region. This is due to the more streamlined shape of the oval-shaped capsule, which allows the airflow to reattach more smoothly after separation from the outer surface, thereby minimizing the size and intensity of turbulence in the wake region. Elongated capsules can promote more laminar flow patterns and have fewer shades of color patterns than cylindrical capsules. Strong turbulence leads to reduced heat convection, thereby reducing battery heat dissipation. A larger major radius/minor radius ratio (
r
ma , i
/
r
mi , i
) can make the capsule more streamlined, thereby reducing turbulence in the wake region and enhancing heat dissipation capabilities.

The temperature profile of the PCM layer in battery capsules is presented in Fig. 3. The color coding indicates temperature levels, with darker shades corresponding to lower temperatures. For the cylindrical battery capsule depicted in Fig. 3a, a gradual increase in the inner surface radius of the outer sheath correlates with an expansion of the PCM layer. The progression from lighter to darker shades with increasing radius suggests that a more extensive PCM layer results in better heat absorption due to a larger inner radius. This is because the greater volume of PCM can absorb more heat before reaching its phase change temperature, which is advantageous for thermal management. The oval-shaped battery capsule shows different temperature distributions in Fig. 3b–d. As the difference between the major and minor axes increases from 1 mm to 3 mm, the color deepens significantly, especially at the end of the major radius (
r
ma , i
). This result indicates that larger oval-shaped capsules have greater heat storage capabilities. A longer main radius leads to a larger PCM layer, which accommodates more PCM, thus prolonging the phase change time of PCM. In Fig. 4 d, the color of the capsule with 16 mm major radius and 13 mm minor radius is obviously darker than other capsules, proving that its temperature is lower than other capsules. Therefore, it is concluded that larger geometric shapes can affect the effect of the PCM layer, thereby affecting the heat dissipation of the entire system.

The experimental setup shown in Fig. 4a is designed to evaluate the heat dissipation capabilities of the developed battery capsule system. Inside the inner sheath is a resistance heater made from copper. A DC power supply (Tekpower TP3005T) delivers 7 V with a current of 1.201 A. This electrical input facilitates the generation of a temperature output of approximately 65 °C by the resistance heater, simulating the heat generated by a battery. Between the inner sheath and outer sheath, there is a PCM layer that acts as a thermal buffer. The bottom case is made of the same material as the battery capsule. As shown in Fig. 7a, thermocouples are positioned at different locations within the battery capsule to monitor temperature variations. All thermocouples are connected to a data logger (Pico TC-08), and the data obtained is displayed on a laptop for real-time monitoring. The thermal camera (FLIR) is set up on the side to capture the geometric view of the battery capsule. A multi-speed level fan is also placed in front of the battery capsule; however, not all experiments involve using the fan. This setup provides a comprehensive system to assess the heat dissipation performance of the battery capsules under various conditions.

The thermal properties were measured using the Hot Disk® Instrument TPS 2500S (Hot Disk, Gothenburg, Sweden) [26]. The copper thermal resistance piece employed in the experiment had a maximum power capacity of 100 W. The heating plate (Cole-Parmer, Vernon Hills, IL, USA) [27] was used to heat the samples. K-type thermocouples and the accompanying thermocouple data logger (Pico Technology, Eaton Socon, UK) [28] were used for temperature measurements and data acquisition. Table 2 presents various materials used for creating PCMs and basic physical properties such as melting point, latent heat, and granule size associated with the material. The NASA Glenn Research Center (Cleveland, OH) provides all materials used in this project.

This research aims to study how the particle size of carbon would affect the effectiveness of PCM under different conditions. In this research, two sizes of carbon particles were used: 100–150 µm Cabot carbon black powder and 19 nm Huber carbon black powder. These carbon particles were individually incorporated into paraffin wax, and their properties were analyzed. The PCM formula [29] contains a variety of ingredients, including paraffin, with a mass ratio of 82.5 %, as well as Huber carbon (7 %), Cabot carbon (4.5 %), and HDPE (6 %). HDPE serves as a support material in PCM formulations, enhancing the structural integrity of the compound. However, adding HDPE results in high viscosity and poor fluidity. As a result, HDPE will not be added in subsequent experiments.

Lower heating and cooling values correspond to an extended phase change period, as indicated in Fig. 5 a. This prolonged phase change duration maintains the battery within the safe operational timeframe for an ample time. The maximum concentration of Huber and Cabot reached approximately 15.5 %. Beyond this threshold, the sample transitioned from a liquid to a paste state. The issue with Cabot carbon arose from its relatively large particle size, posing considerable difficulty in achieving a homogeneous mixture with paraffin wax. Typically, a uniform stirring duration of fifteen minutes at 1000 rpm was required to attain satisfactory blending for the Cabot mixture. In contrast, according to the chart, Huber is easy to mix and demonstrates excellent heating and cooling rate performance. However, a drawback for Huber was the occurrence of sedimentation phenomena after standing. To mitigate this issue, introducing vibrations or tilting during operation proved beneficial [30].

The thermal properties were assessed using the TPS 2500 S instrument listed in Table 3 . The material's heat transfer capability was determined by measuring thermal conductivity [31] and thermal diffusion [32], resulting from a significant temperature gradient. Specific heat [33] is a crucial parameter of PCM due to its direct influence on the duration of phase change. Table 3 shows that different amounts of Huber nano carbon mixed with paraffin wax change the wax's heat handling characteristics slightly. Heat conduction is almost the same across the samples; heat spread varies more, with some samples transferring heat faster than others, and the capacity to store heat also varies, affecting how quickly the material changes from solid to liquid or vice versa. As for storing energy, the samples range from 1.110 to 1.7737 Mj per cubic meter. The third sample may change phases faster because it keeps less energy, while the fifth sample, with the most energy storage, might change phases more slowly.

The temperature profiles of various Huber carbon black samples are presented in Fig. 5b, demonstrating their phase change durations. For each sample, the phase change duration is easily determined. Experimentally, the Cabot carbon black sample showed a 25-minute average phase change duration, while the Huber and Cabot blend exhibited a 31-minute duration. Notably, the Huber carbon alone had a longer duration of 33 min, keeping the battery within a safe operational temperature range. For further experiments, Huber carbon black was chosen due to its superior compatibility with paraffin wax and better heating effects than the Huber and Cabot mix. A longer phase change duration is equivalent to extending the safe use time of the battery. Low concentrations of Huber carbon were deemed optimal for balancing cost and performance. Fig. 5a showed similar heating rates for 2 % and 4 % concentrations. After evaluating phase change durations, the 1 %, 2 %, and 4 % concentrations stood out. For phase change duration, concentrations of Huber at 1 % and 2 % demonstrate superior performance compared to 4 %. Concurrently, in terms of cost-effectiveness, a preference for lower concentrations emerges. As shown in Fig. 5b, in the steady state, a concentration of 2 % has a lower temperature than 1 %, proving that it is suitable for subsequent experimental research.

The Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) diagram provides a detailed examination of the thermal properties of pure paraffin wax and its enhancements with varying concentrations of Huber carbon black. The DSC analysis in Fig. 6 reveals that the phase change material (PCM) enhanced with Huber nano-carbon exhibits a lower heat capacity compared to pure paraffin wax. The inclusion of 1 % to 5 % Huber nano-carbon reduces the latent heat of the PCM, as evident from the DSC curves. Despite this reduction, the thermal conductivity and heat transfer efficiency of the PCM are significantly improved. Pure paraffin wax's peak temperature is at 51.62 °C with a normalized enthalpy of 136.65 J/g, indicating a high latent heat capacity. The 1 % Huber sample shows a peak temperature of 51.3 °C and a normalized enthalpy of 133.79 J/g, slightly lower than the pure wax but still maintaining substantial heat absorption capabilities. The 2 % Huber sample exhibits a peak temperature of 50.99 °C and a normalized enthalpy of 136.76 J/g, closely matching the pure wax and highlighting its optimal balance of latent heat and enhanced thermal conductivity. The 3 % Huber sample has a peak temperature of 51.33 °C and a normalized enthalpy of 130.85 J/g, indicating a slight decrease in latent heat compared to lower Huber concentrations. Similarly, the 4 % Huber sample shows a peak temperature of 51.43 °C with a normalized enthalpy of 132.77 J/g, and the 5 % Huber sample presents a peak temperature of 51.32 °C and a normalized enthalpy of 131.68 J/g. All samples have close onset and offset temperatures, with the onset around 41 °C and the offset around 54 °C. The onset temperature marks the beginning of the phase transition, while the offset temperature indicates the completion of the phase transition. The DSC diagram reveals that the 2 % Huber sample maintains a high latent heat capacity comparable to pure wax while offering improved thermal conductivity, making it an optimal choice for enhancing thermal management systems.

A thermal test was conducted on each battery capsule to explore the cooling concept utilizing PCMs. The Markforged 3D Printer was employed to manufacture the thermal test capsule, using Onyx material with chopped carbon fibers. Following the 3D printing of each capsule, phase-change compound materials were injected into the battery capsule using a plastic pipette. From Fig. 4b, temperature monitoring is conducted at three critical points using thermocouples: Thermocouple 1 is positioned between the resistance heater and the inner sheath to measure the immediate thermal output of the heater. Thermocouple 2 is immersed directly in the PCM layer to monitor the temperature changes as it absorbs and dissipates heat. Finally, thermocouple 3 is connected to the outer sheath to measure the external surface temperature of the system. This arrangement allows comprehensive monitoring of the entire system temperature, facilitating subsequent analysis.

Simulation results show that oval-shaped capsules have better cooling performance. Therefore, this study will prioritize the use of oval-shaped capsules. The experiment encompasses six distinct sizes of samples. Six shapes were fabricated for the 3D printing of these samples, including one cylindrical shape with an outer sheath thickness of 0.8 mm and inner radii of 13 mm. Additionally, five oval designs were created with the same outer sheath thickness, featuring major axis radii (
r
ma , o
)/minor axis radii (
r
mi , o
) of the outer oval surface measuring 14.5/14.0, 15.0/14.0, 16.0/14.0, 17.0/14.0, and 17.0/15.0, respectively (unit: mm). These designs varied in dimensions, including major/minor axis radii and outer sheath thicknesses. However, inherent 3D printing inaccuracies led to deviations between the actual samples and the ideal designs. The cylindrical sample exhibited a measured inner sheath radius of 10 mm and an outer sheath of 13.5 mm. Other five oval-shaped samples displayed measured inner sheath radii, major axis radii of the outer sheath, and minor axis radii of the outer sheath as 10, 14.7, and 13.7 mm; 10, 15.1, and 14.3 mm; 10, 16.2, and 13.8 mm; 10, 16.7, and 14.6 mm; and 10, 17.2, and 14.2 mm, respectively.

In Fig. 7 , the axis ratio of 17.2/14.2 emerges as the most advantageous configuration for the thermal management of battery capsules. This specific ratio demonstrates improved heating rates for the outer sheaths of wax and PCM, indicating enhanced heat distribution capabilities. Despite the variations in heating rates across different capsules, the 17.2/14.2 ratio capsule consistently performs well, especially for the outer sheaths. Combined with the conclusions from the previous simulation analysis (section 2.4), 17.2/14.2 has the largest PCM layer. The results point to the 17.2/14.2 oval-shaped capsule as the superior choice for optimizing thermal management in battery systems.

Air cooling is integral to lithium-ion batteries' hybrid thermal management system. The experimental setup incorporated a multi-speed level fan to simulate realistic conditions, achieving an average wind speed of 2.3 m per second, monitored by an anemometer (HoldPeak HP-866B), with an ambient temperature of 21.2 °C. The wind speed mimics the dynamic operating conditions encountered by drones. Fig. 8 a presents the experimental data comparing a battery capsule filled with 2 % Huber nano carbon PCM to a control sample where the PCM layer is filled with air. The inner sheath temperature, indicative of the battery temperature, and the outer sheath temperature, where heat exchange with the flowing air occurs, were recorded. While the outer sheath temperatures of both samples were similar, the inner sheath temperatures differed significantly; the PCM-filled capsule effectively dissipated heat, maintaining the battery within a safe operating temperature range, whereas the air-filled capsule resulted in battery overheating, underscoring the superior thermal management capability of the PCM design. Additionally, Fig. 8b compares the performance of the PCM-filled capsule with a capsule filled with pure paraffin wax, revealing that although both designs exhibit effective heat dissipation, the PCM design demonstrates higher heat transfer efficiency, leading to lower battery temperatures. This indicates that the PCM-filled design can more effectively manage increased ambient temperatures than the wax-filled design. The integration of passive PCM cooling and active air cooling prevents overheating. It extends the battery's operational life and safety by ensuring it remains within optimal temperature ranges during high-load conditions.

Thermal images captured at six different time intervals are shown in Fig. 9 to analyze the heat dissipation performance of wax-filled and PCM-filled battery capsules under a 2.3 m/s wind flow. The left sample is the wax-filled capsule, while the right is the PCM-filled capsule. Throughout the experiment, the PCM-filled capsule consistently demonstrates superior thermal management capabilities. At the initial stages (35 s to 125 s), both samples exhibit a rapid temperature rise, particularly at the inner sheath, representing the battery temperature. However, the PCM-filled capsule shows a more efficient heat dissipation, maintaining a lower temperature than the wax-filled capsule. By 250 s, the temperature difference between the two samples becomes more pronounced, with the PCM-filled capsule effectively managing the heat and preventing significant temperature rise. Specifically, at 250 s, the inner sheath temperature of the PCM-filled capsule stabilizes around 33 °C, whereas the wax-filled capsule reaches approximately 35 °C. At 400 s, these differences are even more evident; the PCM-filled capsule inner sheath temperature is about 35 °C, while the wax-filled capsule reaches nearly 38 °C. At the steady-state stage (1000 s), the thermal images clearly indicate that the PCM-filled capsule sustains a lower temperature, with the inner sheath temperature around 37 °C, whereas the wax-filled capsule shows an inner sheath temperature close to 41 °C. These observations demonstrate the PCM-filled capsule's higher heat transfer efficiency and better performance in maintaining safe operating temperatures for the battery. Although effective in heat dissipation, the wax-filled capsule shows higher inner sheath temperatures, indicating a lesser ability to manage increased thermal loads.

This research successfully developed and optimized an advanced hybrid heat dissipation system for lithium-ion battery packs, particularly suited for drone applications. The system employs an innovative battery capsule design filled with a PCM compound enhanced with 2 % Huber nano-carbon, significantly improving thermal conductivity and stability. Using Differential Scanning Calorimetry and thermal imaging, the best composition of PCM was selected, ensuring optimal thermal performance. The hybrid cooling system, combining passive PCM cooling with active air cooling, demonstrated superior performance in maintaining battery temperatures within a safe operating range, effectively extending the operational life and enhancing the safety of the batteries. Comparative studies indicated that the PCM-filled capsules outperformed air-filled and pure paraffin wax-filled capsules regarding thermal management efficiency. The study highlighted the geometric advantages of the oval-shaped capsule design, with a major/minor axis ratio of 17.2/14.2 mm, providing optimal heat dissipation. This design ensures efficient thermal regulation, preventing overheating and maintaining consistent battery performance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: [Yi Zheng reports equipment, drugs, or supplies and writing assistance were provided by NASA Glenn Research Center. Yi Zheng reports financial support was provided by National Science Foundation. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper].

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is partially supported by the National Aeronautics and Space Administration Glenn Research Center Faculty Fellowship and the National Science Foundation through grant number CBET-1941743. The authors would like to acknowledge Dr. Jon Goldsby, Dr. Zachary Tuchfeld, and Dr. Meelad Ranaiefar at the NASA Glenn Research Center for their discussion of the 3D-printed battery capsules and heat dissipation data analysis.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simulation of passive thermal management system for lithium-ion battery packs
J. Power Sources
